checks.a_analysis: The output contains only information present in the source document. All headings and body text correspond to text found in the PDF. The title in the metadata is slightly modified for naming conventions but does not represent a content fabrication error within the document body.
There is no contact information in the source PDF, and no fabricated contact information was found in the output JSON.

All three pages of the source PDF are accounted for in the JSON output. Page 1 content is in section 1, Page 2 content (initial table rows) is in section 2, and Page 3 content (remaining table rows and footer) is in section 3.

The two tables spanning pages 2 and 3 are correctly captured. Section 2 contains the first 8 task rows (2.1 through 4.3), and Section 3 contains the remaining 7 task rows (4.4 through 4.10). The row counts match the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: No significant text content was omitted. All goals, objectives, benefits, and task descriptions are fully present in the JSON elements.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content accurately mirrors the source. Minor OCR differences or punctuation handling in the JSON do not change the meaning of the information (e.g., maintaining the asterisks for GICC priority tasks).

checks.a_analysis: The document header and title repeat on each page in the source, and this repetition is faithfully preserved in the output. There is no evidence of unintentional duplication of text blocks within the sections.

Each section maintains a valid heading hierarchy without skipping levels within the same reading flow. While the level of the main title varies between pages (H1 on page 1 vs H2 on page 2), this is an acceptable normalization for multi-page document structure within the JSON.

The complex tables on pages 2 and 3 are correctly converted. Headers are correctly identified, and the 5-column structure of the source is preserved in all rows. Data alignment within cells (such as multi-line status descriptions) is correct.
The reading order follows the source's logical flow (header, title, body content, tables, footers) page by page.

All lists on Page 1 (Goals A-C, Objectives 1-4, Benefits 1-4, Responsibilities 1-2) have been flattened into single paragraph elements. By merging distinct list items into a single block of text separated only by newline characters, the document's structural list markers and semantic structure are lost.
checks.b_evidence_source:
Goals
A. Geospatial datasets managed...
B. Cooperative and collaborative...
C. Geospatial datasets are widely shared...

checks.a_analysis: No links are present in the source PDF, and none were injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. The headings, paragraphs, and tables follow the same logical flow as the original document. Although the HTML layout is a single continuous scroll compared to the multi-page PDF, the relationship between sections and their content remains intact. The conversion to a responsive single-column layout is an expected feature of HTML and does not constitute a layout failure.

The tables on pages 2 and 3 of the source PDF are correctly rendered in the HTML. The conversion maintains the five-column structure. The headers are visually distinct with shading, and the cell contents are correctly aligned within their respective rows and columns. The tables are clearly readable and retain their tabular structure.
The source PDF is entirely text-based and contains no meaningful images, logos, or charts. Therefore, no images were required to be converted to the HTML output.

There is no visual duplication of meaningful body content. The document titles/headers from the top of each PDF page are repeated in the HTML body as it transitions between content from those pages, but this is a standard result of page-by-page conversion and does not involve duplicating paragraphs, list items, or table rows. The table of contents at the top is a navigation aid and is not considered content duplication.
